coredata.dc:title: Fast response ZnO/PVA nanocomposite-based photodiodes modified by graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Mousavi, Seyedeh Soraya
Sajad, Batool
Majlesara, Mohammad Hossein
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a new class of the polymer-based photodetectors is introduced. Poly (vinyl alcohol) is used as the host polymer and ZnO@graphene quantum dots utilized as the filler of the polymer matrix. The specifications of the fabricated photodiodes are improved by exploiting the unique characteristics of graphene quantum dots (GQD). The manufactured photodiode, based on ZnO@GQD/PVA nanocomposite, indicates short rise time of 0.06 s and responsivity of 46.5 AW−1. The enhanced responsivity and transient time compared to ZnO/PVA photodetectors, are attributed to the graphene quantum dot encapsulation. Also, it achieves lower dark current and higher signal to noise ratio due to the creation of a Schottky barrier at the interface of ZnO@GQD core-shell structure. Moreover, the photoluminescence emission of prepared thin films using GQD is significantly increased in the blue and ultraviolet spectral range to make it a potential candidate for the light emitting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc oxide
Core-shell
Graphene quantum dots
PVA-based nanocomposite
Fast response photodiode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the graphene quantum dots

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic design of fabricated device based on the ZnO@GQD/PVA nanocomposite.

SEM micrographs of (a) ZnO nanoparticle, (b) ZnO@GQD core-shell nanoparticles, (c) ZnO@GQD/PVA nanocomposite thin film, (d) TEM Image of graphene quantum dots, (e) The size micrograph of GQD, (f) size distribution of GQD.
A comparison between UV–Vis spectra of ZnO/PVA and ZnO@GQD/PVA.
Photoluminescence spectra of the ZnO, GQD, PVA, ZnO/PVA and ZnO@GQD/PVA thin films.
A comparison between the spectral responsivity of ZnO/PVA and ZnO@GQD/PV.
(a) A comparison between the dark currents of the samples and (b) creation of schottky barrier at the interface of ZnO and GQD.
A comparison between the photocurrents of the samples at the wavelengths with the peak of responsivity.
A comparison between the I-t curves of the photodiodes based on ZnO/PVA and ZnO@GQD/PVA nanocomposites.
Spectral response time of the ZnO@GQD/PVA photodiode at different wavelengths.
The specification of the fabricated photodiode based on ZnO@GQD/PVA nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Fast response ZnO/PVA nanocomposite-based photodiodes modified by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photonics Laboratory, Department of Physics, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanophotonics Laboratory, Applied Science Research Center, Kharazmi University, Alborz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photonics Laboratory, Department of Physics, Kharazmi University, Alborz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a new class of the polymer-based photodetectors is introduced. Poly (vinyl alcohol) is used as the host polymer and ZnO@graphene quantum dots utilized as the filler of the polymer matrix. The specifications of the fabricated photodiodes are improved by exploiting the unique characteristics of graphene quantum dots (GQD). The manufactured photodiode, based on ZnO@GQD/PVA nanocomposite, indicates short rise time of 0.06 s and responsivity of 46.5 AW−1. The enhanced responsivity and transient time compared to ZnO/PVA photodetectors, are attributed to the graphene quantum dot encapsulation. Also, it achieves lower dark current and higher signal to noise ratio due to the creation of a Schottky barrier at the interface of ZnO@GQD core-shell structure. Moreover, the photoluminescence emission of prepared thin films using GQD is significantly increased in the blue and ultraviolet spectral range to make it a potential candidate for the light emitting devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, polymer-based photovoltaic devices have attracted great attention of the scientists [1]. Similar to other novel technologies, high-quality polymeric device manufacturing is challenging. Lots of researches have attempted to improve the performance of these devices by exploiting the unique properties of nanocomposites [2–6]. A nanocomposite material composes of a polymer or copolymer having nanoparticles dispersed in the polymer matrix. It offers new opportunities for fabricating the next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices. Features of nanocomposite materials depend on properties of their individual constituents, morphology and interfacial characteristics [7]. Interfacial interactions between the filler and the matrix improve dielectric, optical and electrical properties of composites. PVA (Polyvinyl alcohol Alcohol) has several attractive physical properties, including elegant film forming, biocompatibility, dopant-dependent electrical and optical properties. Therefore, PVA is a potential candidate for fabricating devices with enhanced performance [8–10]. A special kind of nanocomposite composed of zinc oxide (ZnO) suits well for electro-optical devices. ZnO possesses many superior properties such as good transparency, high electron mobility and direct wide band gap (3.37 eV at room temperature) [11,12]. Therefore, it can be used in a wide range of applications in the blue/UV region of optoelectronic devices [13–15]. Despite high electron-mobility of ZnO (155 cm2 V−1 s−1) [16], most photonic ZnO/PVA-based devices suffer from the low speed of response [17,18]. On the other hand, carbon-based materials have extraordinary properties, thus finding applications as additives to various structural and functional materials [19]. When they are incorporated into a polymer matrix, the resulting composites have significantly different properties than the original. Graphene as a two dimensional one atom thick allotrope of carbon exhibits excellent electronic properties: high carrier mobility, ballistic transport, quantum Hall effect at room temperature, and good mechanical and chemical stability [20,21]. Therefore, it is considered as promising material particularly for nanoelectronic and optoelectronic devices such as photodetectors, solar cells [20–26], in bio- and chemical sensors [27–29], quantum computing [30], etc. Compared to graphene, GQD affords substantially more oxygen-containing functional groups on the planar surface; thus, they can serve as highly active reaction sites. In addition, GQD has excellent solubility, tunable band gap, low cytotoxicity and high luminesce [31,32]. In particular, high conductivity and large specific surface-area of GQD in conjunction with various active sites on the planar surface makes them a desirable and multifarious platform to integrate with diverse nanostructured materials for photovoltaic devices [33]. Accordingly, we introduce a novel kind of nanocomposite, modified by graphene quantum dots offering high transparency and low resistance thin films for manufacturing fast photovoltaic devices. In this work, ZnO nanoparticles were wrapped with GQD to form ZnO@GQD core-shells and used as the filler of PVA. Then, nanocomposite thin films were used as photoactive layers of blue/ultraviolet photodetector.

ZnO nanoparticles are synthesized through sol-gel route described in reference [34]. According to the procedure, Zn (CH3COO)2·2H2O (99.5% Merck), Triethylamine (TEA) (99% Merck) and absolute ethanol (99% Merck) are employed as the precursor, surfactant and solvent, respectively. The concentration of zinc ions in the solution is kept constant at 1 mol/L. For this aim, zinc acetate dihydrate is dissolved in a mixture of ethanol and TEA under vigorous magnetic stirring. The molar ratio of TEA to zinc acetate dihydrate is adjusted to 2:5. A colorless, homogeneous and transparent solution is obtained after ~30 min. To prepare the powder, the resulting transparent sols are kept for the aging process to complete the gelation and hydrolysis process. After a while, the white ZnO precipitates were slowly crystallized and settled down at the bottom of the flask. The white precipitates dried at 120 °C for 1 h in an oven to vaporize the solvent. Then, it was annealed in a furnace at 850 °C for 2 h to remove the organic residuals and achieve the high crystal structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: GQD is synthesized by directly pyrolyzing citric acid, through the method suggested by Dong and et al. [35]. According to the procedure, 2 g citric acid (99%, Sigma Aldrich) is put into a 5 mL beaker and heated to 200̊ C. After 5 min, the citric acid powder is liquated. The obtained orange liquid is neutralized to pH 7.0 by adding NaOH solution under vigorous stirring.

In order to prepare the ZnO@GQD core-shell, 0.1 g as-synthesized ZnO nanoparticles are added into the 5 mL as-synthesized GQD suspension. It is stirred for 2 h to be properly mixed. The mixture is then dried at 50 °C overnight in an oven. The obtained powder is then annealed at 150 °C for an hour.

Initially, a PVA solution is prepared by adding 0.5 g PVA (99%, Merck) to 50 mL DI water. The mixture is stirred vigorously at 85 °C for 5 h until a homogeneous and clear solution was obtained. The sols are prepared with the optimized amounts of ZnO-based dopant (4 wt%) [35]. Then, 4 wt%, ZnO and ZnO@GQD powders are added to the PVA solution and mixed for 2 h. Then, they are ultrasonicated repeatedly to obtain the homogeneously dispersed solutions of ZnO/PVA and ZnO@GQD/PVA.

Prior to the coating thin films, the prepared Si wafers are first cleaned ultrasonically and then cleaned according to the RCA-1 regulation that is a standard set of wafer cleaning steps. Then thin films are prepared by spin coating in two steps; first at 1000 rpm for 10 s and then at 5000 rpm for 30 s. Some parts of the deposited layers have been removed through a simple step of lithography. The prepared thin films are annealed at 150 °C for an hour. The electrical contacts are created using the Ag paste.

The fabricated devices are then analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) and electro-optical measurements. Fig. 1 illustrates a schematic design of the fabricated device using ZnO@GQD/PVA thin film.

Morphology and structure of the deposited nanocomposite thin film and its components are studied using SEM and TEM micrographs. Fig. 2 shows the structures of the prepared components of the last nanocomposite thin film in each step of preparation. According to the Fig. 2-a, most of the ZnO nanoparticles are about 50 nm in size. Fig. 2-b illustrates the core-shell structure of ZnO@GQD. It is observed that the size of particles is increased after wrapping the ZnO nanoparticles with graphene quantum dots.

The morphology and size distribution of the GQD can be determined based on the Fig. 2-d, e, and f. The size of graphene quantum dots ranges from 1 to 5 nm with 2.5 nm average size. Moreover, the SEM image of PVA-ZnO@GQD shows a highly uniform thin film containing the core-shell nanoparticles smaller than 100 nm in size (Fig. 2-c).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the ZnO/PVA and ZnO@GQD/PVA nanocomposites are measured at the same concentration for the accurate comparison. The absorbance spectra of the ZnO/PVA nanocomposite films are containing the fundamental peak of the PVA and ZnO (as shown in Fig. 3 ). As Mansour et al. reported for a native PVA, one broad absorbance band in the range of 250–290 nm and one weak band or shoulder at 330 nm are assigned to the electronic transitions π → π* and n → π* [36]. While the peak in the range of 380–385 nm (3.23 eV) corresponds to π → π* electronic excitation in impregnated ZnO [37].

Although the spectrum of ZnO@GQD/PVA shows higher absorption compared to that of ZnO/PVA, but the absorption peaks related to the ZnO are approximately the same for both samples. It can be attributed to the higher numbers of ZnO molecules. Moreover, an extra peak in the range of 320–350 nm can be observed which corresponds to either the n → π∗ transition of CO bonds of GQD or n → π* transition of PVA [38].

The photoluminescence spectra of the samples composed of PVA, ZnO, GQD, ZnO/PVA, and ZnO@GQD/PVA thin films is depicted in Fig. 4 . All the samples were excited with the light at 320 nm wavelength. The emission spectrum of the ZnO/PVA nanocomposite thin films shows a higher intensity compared to the neat PVA. Incorporation of the ZnO into the PVA through CH2–CH (OH) bonding of the compound can enhance the stability of the molecular orbital electron density. The stable electron density increases the number of effective transitions; thus, the intensity of the fluorescence enhances [39]. In addition, an extra ultraviolet peak at 388 nm in the ZnO/PVA spectrum is observed due to the near band emission of the ZnO nanoparticles (at 388 nm) settled in the PVA matrix [40,41]. The spectrum is also blue-shifted relative to the PVA thin film (Fig. 4).

According to the high annealing temperature in ZnO nanoparticles preparation, its photoluminescence spectrum shows a peak in the range of 430 to 450 nm, due to the defects created during the annealing process. Moreover, the peaks of PL spectra in the ranges of 430-450 nm and 500 to 550 nm show the zinc interstitials and oxygen vacancies defects, respectively [13,31,42].

Therefore, the emission enhancement observed in the spectrum of the ZnO/PVA thin films compared to the neat PVA attributed to the overlapping emissions of both PVA and the related emissions of ZnO. The most interesting point observable in the photoluminescence spectrum of the ZnO@GQD/PVA thin film is that the intensity of the sample enhanced significantly by introducing the graphene quantum dots in the nanocomposite. It is due to the fact that the GQD possesses a graphene core and numbers of attached chemical groups.

Both the graphene core and the surrounding chemical groups can control the photoluminescence behavior of its compounds. Specifically, its core determines the intrinsic emission, while the attached chemical groups control the surface states [29]. It is also highly fluorescent because of the extended π-conjugation structure of carbon atoms.

The excellent fluorescence emission of GQD relates to both quantum confinement and boundary effects [43–45]. Moreover, the new chemical bonds, created between the GQD-attached groups and ZnO defects, result in an intense emission of the nanocomposite films. As Fig. 4 indicates, the peak emission of GQD is located at 445 nm corresponding to the band energy of 2.7 eV.

The spectral response of the photodiodes is calculated by Eq. (1) for the different wavelengths located in the range of 365–450 nm under the light illumination of 500 μW/Cm2 by light emission diodes (LEDs).
(1)
R =
I light
−
I Dark
p inc
where ILight is the current under the illumination, IDark is the dark current, Pinc is the incident illumination power on the effective area [14].

The results indicated that ZnO@GQD/PVA thin film presents higher responsivity in the whole range except at the wavelength of 385 nm in which the responsivity of the ZnO/PVA is surpassed (Fig. 5 ). It is related to the higher amounts of ZnO in the ZnO/PVA than ZnO@GQD/PVA because both of the samples were prepared with 4 wt% filler powder. On the other hand, the peak response of ZnO@GQD/PVA shows much higher intensity than that of ZnO/PVA.

The amounts of responsivity about 46.5 AW−1 at the wavelength of 420 nm is measured for the GQD-based photodiode. It was about 19.6 AW−1 for ZnO/PVA sample under the light illumination of 385 nm wavelength.

Comparison of the dark currents of the samples indicates that decorating the ZnO nanoparticles with GQD leads to a considerable reduction of dark current down to 0.7 μA (Fig. 6a). It could be related to the fact that the differences between the work functions of ZnO and GQD lead to the creation of a schottky barrier at the interface. Accordingly, the charge carriers cannot overcome the barrier at room temperature (Fig. 6b).

The photocurrents of both samples are measured at the different wavelengths but for the better comparison, their graph is plotted at the peak response of each sample. The results are calculated as the following equation:
(1)
I ph
=
I light
−
I dark
in which Idark and Ilight are the measured dark current and the current under the light illumination, respectively [14].

According to the spectral responsivity results, the photocurrents are recorded at the peak position of 385 nm and 420 nm for ZnO/PVA and ZnO@GQD/PVA samples, respectively. Based on the results, while the highest amount of 1.3 μA is recorded for ZnO/PVA photodiode, the higher amount of photocurrent ~2.3 μA is achieved for ZnO@GQD/PVA. It is noteworthy that inserting graphene quantum dots with the higher electron density as well as higher electron mobility can improve the electrical characteristics of the nanocomposites. The enhanced photocurrent of ZnO@GQD/PVA is attributed to the charge transfer from GQD to ZnO.

Furthermore, high band gap energy of the prepared GQD makes it suitable for UV and near UV photonics applications. It is one of the most important advantages of GQD over the graphene [19]. The amounts of photocurrent at different wavelengths are listed in Table 1 .

It should be also noticed that the observed noise of the dark and photocurrents belongs to the photodiode composed of GQD, is obviously lower than those of pure ZnO (Fig. 7 ). Signal to noise ratios (SNR) of 18.6 and 4.0 are calculated for ZnO@GQD/PVA and ZnO/PVA, respectively. It can be attributed to the existence of a Schottky barrier between the ZnO and GQD interfaces that reduces the dark current and the other fluctuations which can produce the noise. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) of the photodetectors is also calculated using the following equation:
(2)
EQE =
I ph
e
p in
hυ
= R
hc eλ
where hν is the energy of the incident photon and R is the photoresponsivity of the UV photodetector [13]. Accordingly, the EQE of ZnO@GQD/PVA and ZnO/PVA are calculated about 137.4 and 63.1, respectively. It is obvious that higher responsivity results in higher amounts of EQE. Improving the speed of the polymer-based photodiodes is the main goal of this research. For this aim, we have tried to produce a nanocomposite which can offer the higher electron mobility and the lower resistance. Therefore, wrapping ZnO with GQD seems to be the best solution for this challenge because of the high electron density and mobility. Four point probes measurement was carried out to determine the sheet resistance. The low amount of resistance about 844 Ω/sq. is recorded for the PVA thin film filled with ZnO@GQD nanoparticles. While the higher amounts of 2.3 kΩ/sq. and 3.1 kΩ/sq. are measured for ZnO/PVA and PVA layers, respectively. The results confirm that the larger number of electrons reduces the resistance of the thin film by introducing GQD.

Moreover, the response time of the devices is measured by turning on and turning off the UV light emitting diodes at different wavelengths. The I-t curves of both samples at the wavelengths with the highest responsivity are plotted according to the Fig. 8 . As it was expected, a transient response time of 0.06 s is measured for the ZnO@GQD/PVA photodiode at 450 nm. While the response time of the ZnO/PVA photodiode was about 0.35 s.

The I-t curves of the ZnO@GQD/PVA samples for the measured wavelengths are illustrated in Fig. 9 . The recorded rise times and other specifications are listed in Table 1. These response times appear to be the lowest reported so far. According to the literatures, for the ZnO/PVA-based photodetectors the response times are reported in the order of few seconds [38,46,47].

In this paper, fabrication of a high-speed polymer-based photodiode with the enhanced responsivity is reported. The device produced using a novel kind of the nanocomposite composed of ZnO@GQD core-shell. The high response of 46.5 A/W, the low rise time of 0.06 s and high SNR about 18.6 are recorded for this photodiode. A fast response about 0.35 s recorded for ZnO/PVA photodetector which is also faster than the reported similar photodetectors so far. Furthermore, the thin films composed of the introduced nanocomposite show enhanced emissions in the blue and ultraviolet region of the spectrum due to the intrinsic emission of graphene quantum dots and its attached functional groups.
